stem: A hospital uses a stress ECG test to screen for coronary artery disease. The test has a sensitivity of 87% and a specificity of 80%. The prevalence of coronary artery disease in the patient population is estimated to be between 6.4% and 14.1%, with uncertainty due to regional variation and demographic differences in the hospital's catchment area. A patient receives a positive test result. What is the probability that the patient actually has coronary artery disease? Provide your answer as a probability with a confidence interval reflecting the uncertainty in prevalence.

ground_truth.answer: P(coronary artery disease | positive test) ranges from 0.229 to 0.417 as prevalence varies from 6.4% to 14.1%. Point estimate at 10.2% prevalence: 0.331.

solution_steps: Apply Bayes' theorem: P(D|+) = P(+|D)×P(D) / [P(+|D)×P(D) + P(+|¬D)×P(¬D)], where P(+|¬D) = 1-specificity = 0.2.
The confidence interval [0.229, 0.417] reflects the range of posterior probabilities over plausible prevalence values.
common_failure_modes: Confusing sensitivity (87%) with positive predictive value
Providing only a point estimate without acknowledging the prevalence uncertainty
Ignoring the base rate (prevalence) and using the test sensitivity as the answer
Not recognizing the strong base rate effect when prevalence is low
